speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. Today is March 12, 1996. I am Mark Goldberg, the interviewer, interviewing-- interviewing Chief Prosecutor William Dowdell Denson. We're in Lawrence, New York, in the United States. The language is English. How did you get from West Point to Europe to play some role in the judge advocate's court? This is prior to the trials, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got there physically by boat. I left [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] West Point and went down to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the Pier 80 and got on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that boat took me and 10,000 others on over to England. Actually, I think we stopped in Gourock, Scotland, and then went by train from there down to [INAUDIBLE] in the south of England. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did this-- how did this come about? Why weren't you-- why didn't you simply resign and go back to Birmingham to practice law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get sent to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ordered. I received orders to join headquarters US Third Army in Europe. And that came about, I think, through the interests of one of my classmates, who had also been instructed at West Point while I was there, by the name of Colonel [? EM ?] [? O'Connell. ?] He was in the judge advocate's section of headquarters Third Army. And the judge advocate of Third Army was Colonel Charles E. Cheever, whom I had known and whom had been an acting professor of law at West Point when I was there teaching. So I knew Colonel Cheever and Colonel O'Connell. And they asked for my services in Headquarters Third Army. So that's how I got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea of the kind of work that you might do before you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it would be something in the Administration of Military Justice, because I had done a terrific amount of trial work, not only down at my father's office, but when I got to West Point, I think they tallied it up, out of 97 court-martial cases that they had-- and these did not include-- it did include-- but the 97 cases really were involving the soldiers who were manning the detachments, like the field-artillery detachment, the infantry detachment. In other words, they had detachments of every branch of the service there-- stationed there at West Point. And they had their problems, of course. And during my stay there at West Point, out of 97 cases that they had, I participated in 93 of them, either as a trial judge advocate, defense counsel, or law member on general court-martial cases. So I had had a tremendous amount of trial work. I tried a large number of cases for my father. So that trial was really my book.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to-- to Germany-- to-- was it to Erlangen that you reported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you reported to Erlangen, before the trials, before the war-crimes trials, what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Administration of Military Justice, which meant supervising to a certain extent the actions of the court martials in the various units that were in Third Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, these were military courts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Military court martial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Summary court martials, general court martials--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. General court martial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever kinds of court martials that occurred.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point were you invited to serve as chief prosecutor, and how did that come about? What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the summer of '45-- this was after the war was over. At that time, Third Army was in the-- in Bavaria. And the rear echelon was in Munich. The advanced echelon was in Bad Tolz. And it was during that period that Third Army was receiving reports from the War Crimes Investigative Branch, which teams-- what teams had followed the combat troops and investigated those atrocities that were uncovered as the troops advanced. And they-- reports were sent to headquarters Third Army of those that arose in that area. And I was reading those and became familiar with the facts that there was such thing as concentration camps and that there were places of horror and degradation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did you get invited to play a personal role in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say it was in July. July or early August of 1945, when all of the judge advocates were invited to send their representatives to Wiesbaden to be interviewed to determine about who was gonna try the first case. I went to Wiesbaden and came back, and I was informed by my boss, Colonel Cheever, that I had been selected. And that's how I knew that I was gonna participate in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At that point, what was your rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a lieutenant colonel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you expected that you were going to be now the chief prosecutor at just one trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which trial was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Dachau, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau, you got to remember, was located just 17 kilometers north and west of Munich. And we-- the Third Army was there in Munich, the rear echelon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go about preparing? How did you choose your team? How did you get ready for this difficult job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I utilized the reports of the war-crimes teams that had been coming in. And, actually, Seventh Army was the army that liberated Dachau. And they had a war-crimes team there under the command of Colonel Chavez, whose brother was Senator Chavez from New Mexico. And Colonel Chavez and his  men filed a magnificent report, setting forth the different facets of the operations of the Dachau camp. And using their reports as a guide, we started investigating and preparing for the trial of the Dachau concentration camp case.

speaker: interviewer
text: First thing we had to do was decide on a charge. What was it that-- how could you frame this charge? And there were a lot of problems in that connection. Because these camps had been going on since 1933. Well, we didn't have any jurisdiction until the war-- till we ended the war and after December the 7th. And these camps were being tried as war crimes, their operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And war crimes, of course, are defined as those that are in violations of the laws and usages of war. And the United States was participating after December the 7th. So the limits time-wise, so far as the time was concerned, was December 7th, 1941. That's when the United States had jurisdiction, because that's when they came into the war. And they were a combatant from that time on. Now, we would go through the documents that had been accumulated there in Munich and tried to sift out and see what conduct was available to bring to the attention of the court. And knowing that war crimes were committed when there were violations of the laws and usages of war, we had to frame our charges to come within the limitations of our jurisdictional power. And that we did. And that, of course, involved language that was appropriate. And we had problems to the extent that not all the people served in any one place, any one camp, the whole time. There was a turnover. Well, it would be hard to charge them with conspiracy, because it would be hard to prove a-- an agreement, for example, between the SS, who were the guardians at the camps-- and when I say SS, I mean, actually, they were what was known as the Totenkopfverbande, the Death Head Units of the SS. And kapos-- kapos were habitual-- usually-- habitual German prisoners. In Germany at that time, when a prisoner had served his time in the penitentiary and was eligible for release, he wasn't sent back to the community. He was sent to the concentration camp. So that these habitual German criminals were tapped as a source of leadership, in the sense that they were the lowest bases in the leadership operations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that sense, the kapos were like the foreman or the leader of a work gang with this important exception-- the kapo had the power of life and death over the prisoners who were working in his group. Well, as I said, because of the period of time and the physical locations of the different camps and sub-camps that made up the camp-- like Dachau had 160-odd sub-camps. Mauthausen, even more. But you couldn't really prove a conspiracy, so that was out. Well, what collective crime have they committed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what they did was to-- and this was done through the help of the officers at Wiesbaden and also Lord Justice Wright, who was the Lord Chancellor of England. Everybody got together, and they decided on, all right, we can come within the ambit of a common design, and common design to subject these prisoners to beatings, tortures, killings, starvation. Because there was a common intent. There was an intent on the part of those people who operated these camps, from the kommandant down to the kapo, including the guards, to subject these prisoners to extermination, ultimately, where there were Jews. And the Jews were not-- they were singled out for extermination, but they were not the only prisoners in the camps. There were-- actually, when Hitler overran a country, the first thing he did was to collect up the intelligentsia of that country and those who were in authority and those who really were the brains of that particular country that had been overrun and haul them off to concentration camps for protective custody. So that-- you-- we had to have a charge that was broad enough to encompass all these different activities and yet be narrow enough to put them on notice of what it was they were being tried for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does this theory of common design require participation or just knowledge and awareness?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Aiding, abetting, and furthering anyone who participated to the extent of giving aid or abetting or furthering the plan or scheme to subject these prisoners to beatings, killings, and tortures would be within the ambit of the charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. To make it concrete, if I'm a guard outside the door and I know someone inside is being beaten-- I-- I have knowledge of it, but I'm not doing the beating-- am I also guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: You are indeed a participant. And they used to have a concept that you would be principals in the first, second degree, and so forth. That was abolished by federal statute. And when I talk about abolished by federal statute, the statute law of this country is embodied in the process of enforcing war crimes, because that's the standard of this particular combatant. And when the different countries entered into the conventions, the Hague Convention of 1907 and the Geneva Convention of 1929, Germany was also a signatory to that convention. And those two conventions-- the-- the Hague Convention really concerned itself with the duty on the part of combatants toward the civilian population. And the Geneva Convention concerned itself with combatants' treatments of their enemy, other combatants. So that we had two particulars to the charge which was for violation of the laws and usages of war. One embodied the requirements on the country with respect to combatants and-- on the Geneva Convention, and-- with respect to noncombatants under the Hague Convention. And in that way, we put together a charge which was used to bring these individuals to the bar of justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your team. What-- who were the-- I don't mean necessarily name by name, but what was the team like? The prosecution? What-- what resources did you have to gather evidence, gather prisoners, gather witnesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Witnesses. I'm glad you raised that point. Witnesses came from every country in Europe. And there were some Americans that were in the camps, too. And that meant that those that were able to, after the armistice, they went back home and scattered. So we were given the means for ferrying these people back to Dachau. And we had accommodations there in Dachau for the witnesses that were away from home. And it was quite a chore and quite an undertaking to collect these witnesses, because those that were physically able did try to go back to their homes, and they'd scattered all over Europe. And we had a plane that was available to us-- a DC-3 that we used to ferry witnesses from different parts of Europe down to Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of staff did you have? Were there ten people? Were there 100 people? I'm talking about the whole staff now, everyone from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: From your attorney to the pilot, the guards who guarded the prisoners. What-- what sort of resources did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- so far as guarding was concerned, we had the London Poles. The London Poles were not the Communist Poles. And we had a whole flock of London Poles there who did police duty. And so far as the individuals, the interrogators and the investigators, they were Americans, and some of them-- we had some from Holland and different countries that we employed. And so far as numbers were concerned, what I tried to do was assemble a group that was compatible with each other, each member was, and had the brains and the know-how to try a case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of your chief assistants, the one or two people you relied on?

words.text: or two people you relied on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had in Dachau, I had a gentleman by the name of Peter Heller. I had Paul Goethe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bill Lyons. And McKusky from Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you had whatever resources you needed, secretarial help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We-- we-- we-- we had that, but it was all within the limits of the pocketbook which was watched over by the War Crimes Branch of-- of the European Theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you determine which individuals to try? There were hundreds and thousands, of course. How did you select them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll have to explain that this way. The purpose of these trials was to obtain a judicial determination that these camps were criminal operations in their entirety, from the kommandant on down to the kapo. And in that sense, we felt that it was necessary to have a representative from each segment of the camp's operation. Like, for example, we have representatives from the guards who did the work that you mentioned a moment ago. And we had doctors who were from a medical department. We had representatives from the Gestapo that had a branch there in the camp. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] every-- as I say, every activity was represented. So we had to have a large number of accused. In Dachau, for example, we had 40 accused. In Mauthausen, 61. That was a bigger camp. In Flossenburg, there were 45 accused. And in Buchenwald, 31. So the purpose was to obtain a judicial determination of the criminality of this operation. And the basis for that, the reason for that, was that we had over 3,500 alleged war criminals in the War Crimes cage there at Dachau. Well, you couldn't give a full-dressed hearing, as was given in the trial of the cases-- camp cases, proper, which we call the mother case-- to each one of these individuals. We'd still be trying those cases at this time. So what the plan was, and it was considered to be fair, was to get the-- a judicial determination of the criminality of the operation and then use that record of trial presented to subsequent tribunals, in which they would then accept as established the criminality of the operation. And the accused would be those persons who had served there and had participated in the furthering of the common design that was present. And in that way, they could cut down on the length of time for the trial. The accused was not presumed to be guilty, but he had the burden of going forward with proof to acquit himself when it was shown that he was there participating in the operation of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in the original trial, where you had 30 to 60 people you were going to prosecute--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at each camp, did you select these 30 to 60 people based on how notorious they were or how representative they were of each dimension of the camp? Or some other-- was some other criteria used?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have put your finger on it. They were really on the basis of-- of being representatives of the operations that had occurred. And in that way, we were able to get a cross-section of-- of a proper picture of what transpired in each of these camps. Now, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we had problems. One man over here, he only killed 10 people. Another accused over here, he killed 110. Both of them were equally guilty of the offense of violating the laws and usages of war, a war crime. Each one of them could be hanged, because that was the maximum punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how you gonna do? This man killed 10. What are you gonna do? Give him only one 10th of the punishment that you give the  man who had killed 100? So the problem that the prosecution had was trying to equalize the testimony before the court, so that there would be some degree of uniformity in the sentences that were imposed. OK. We'll have to stop there, and we'll continue on the next tape.
